text: Cities and Climate Change: Mitigation and Adaptation (I, II and III) Cities - home to 55% of the global population, or 4.2 billion people - are responsible for up to 70% of human-caused fossil fuel emissions and therefore have a crucial role in addressing climate change across sectors. 11.270x Cities and Climate Change: Mitigation and Adaptation explores all of these areas with critical examination of the strategies cities have already deployed, enabling course participants to investigate urban climate change actions and develop policy recommendations for adaptation and mitigation response. This instructor-paced course is equal parts information assimilation and hands-on application. Learners will gain a deeper understanding of fundamental theories of urban social and economic governance and evaluate these from the standpoint of climate resilience. Learners will improve their understanding of the operation of actors and systems involved in structuring policy and climate action. Through the perspectives of leading academics and practitioners, students will gain fundamental knowledge on core topics in urban climate mitigation and adaptation, including urban climate science, global warming, energy and transportation systems, environmental justice and equity, governance and policy, Traditional Ecological Knowledge (TEK), land use planning, urban design, disaster risk management, among others. Building on and working with the Urban Climate Change Research Network’s (UCCRN’s) scientific community, learners will hear from climate change experts in different disciplines to understand how challenges and opportunities are experienced across the world. They will apply insights gleaned in class to explore city-specific research on climate resilience strategies. This course offers a foundation of knowledge on cities and climate change, with the opportunity to apply what is learned through city case study research for climate policy and action.

11.270x Cities and Climate Change: Mitigation and Adaptation Core Instructors:
- Janelle Knox-Hayes, Professor of Economic Geography and Planning, MIT
- Cynthia Rosenzweig, Senior Research Scientist, NASA Goddard Institute for Space Studies (GISS), Columbia Climate School
- Juan Camilo Osorio, Assistant Professor, Graduate Center for Planning and the Environment, Pratt Institute Course Manager:
- Faizan Jawed Siddiqi, Lecturer and Digital Learning Fellow, MIT DUSP Course Structure:
This course is organized into three 4-week modules, each containing a series of video learning units with topics presented by subject experts. At the end of each module, students in the certificate track complete a comprehensive assignment on their case study city. These assignments support an investigation of Climate Action Plans (CAPs), with the potential to actively support and contribute to a UCCRN Regional Hub's climate action strategy. Required text:
Rosenzweig, C., Solecki, W. D., Romero-Lankao, P., Mehrotra, S., Dhakal, S., & Ibrahim, S. A. (Eds.). (2018). Climate change and cities: Second assessment report of the urban climate change research network. Cambridge University Press. (Abbreviated ARC3.2) Learning Objectives:
- Gain a deeper understanding of fundamental theories of governance and planning impacting urban climate mitigation and adaptation. - Comprehend the relationships between multi-level governance and multi-level climate risk assessment. - Improve understanding of the operation of actors and systems involved in structuring policy and urban planning in response to climate change. - Articulate and advocate for the best practices in climate mitigation and adaptation planning in local urban contexts. Course Schedule: Module 1: Week 1 - Cities and Climate Change
- Urban climate science
- Political economy of climate change
- Mitigation vs. adaptation
- Introduction to climate governance and policy
- Equitable resilience Week 2 - Urban Climate Change Impacts
- Climate disasters and risk
- Environmental justice and equity
- Indigenous environmental planning
- Traditional ecological knowledge Week 3 - Urban Energy and Transportation
- Energy systems
- Transportation and governance
- Urban infrastructure
- Mitigation and adaptation strategies

Week 3 - Urban Energy and Transportation
- Energy systems
- Transportation and governance
- Urban infrastructure
- Mitigation and adaptation strategies Week 4 - Housing, Maladaptation and Social Dimensions of Climate Change
- Housing and informality
- Vulnerability and maladaptation
- Just Transition Module 2: Week 5 - Climate Action
- 6 pathways to urban transformation
- Urban form and function
- Urban planning and design Week 6 - Nature, Environmental Protection and Climate Change
- Transformative adaptation and mitigation
- Urban water systems
- Urban coastal zones
- Urban case studies Week 7 - Urban Inhabitants and Health
- Urban health
- Health risks and impacts
- Mitigating urban health vulnerabilities
- Urban heat island effect Week 8 - Case Studies
- UCCRN case study docking station
- New York City
- Kano, Nigeria
- Copenhagen, Denmark Module 3: Week 9 - The Climate Action Plan (CAP)
- Planning for climate change
- Zooming in on the 6 pathways to urban transformation
- Engaging industry for climate action Week 10 - Components of a Climate Action Plan
- Components of Climate Action Plans
- Cape Town, South Africa
- Ahmedabad, India Week 11 - Drafting a Climate Action Plan
- SWOT analysis and policy recommendation
- Mitigation and adaptation strategies
- Canberra, Australia
- Manila, Philippines Week 12 - Bringing together a Climate Action Plan Prospectus
- CAP role in mitigation and adaptation planning
- Emerging directions for climate action in cities Course Assessment:
Weekly engagement activities include forum discussions, comprehension questions, and short quizzes. Certificate track students complete case study research assignments at the end of each module, assessed through peer review or summarized instructor feedback. Weekly time commitment: 4-6 hours per week. AskTIM Tutor:
AskTIM is a problem-solving socratic AI tutor for the MIT Learn online learning platform. AskTIM's purpose is to help a learner work through a given exercise from their course. Learners can start texting AskTIM from an exercise page on MIT Learn by clicking on the corresponding button on. AskTIM's interface lives on a different platform integrated to MIT Learn through an iframe. The context of the exercise is already known to AskTIM and the interaction remains in the scope of this exercise.

AskTIM's interface lives on a different platform integrated to MIT Learn through an iframe. The context of the exercise is already known to AskTIM and the interaction remains in the scope of this exercise. The learner is expected to do the work and present their solution attempts to the tutor. The mode of interaction is different from regular AI chatbots, that act as assistants. AskTIM can lead the interaction and ask the learner to performs tasks rather than the other way around. AskTIM has been developed in a way that benefits the learning process and does not remove all effort from the learner to avoid reliance. We encourage using AskTIM rather than other available chatbots to ensure long-term learning, even if using askTIM is more demanding.

Network. JANELLE KNOX-HAYES: This course is

with a focus on understanding civic action, governance
and policy deliberation, and strategic change

and access; disaster risk and resilience; health, energy,
and transportation; housing; ecological change;
coastal transformation; and more. CYNTHIA ROSENZWEIG: The main learning objective
of this course is for students to learn, assess, mobilize,

study of their choice. It also links to sustainable development goal
number 11, for inclusive, safe, resilient,
and sustainable cities. JANELLE KNOX-HAYES: The course is structured
into three four week modules. The first seeks to understand climate change; the second
to assess climate action and mobilize actors;
and the third to plan climate action. CYNTHIA ROSENZWEIG: In the first module,

students will examine interactions

on building policy recommendations

the course is taught by a leading international cadre
of urban climate experts. Working with this group of experts to build this class
has been a distinct pleasure for us,
and we hope you enjoy learning from this material
as much as we enjoyed creating it.

JANELLE KNOX-HAYES: Hello. My name is Janelle Knox-Hayes. I'm a professor in the Department of Urban Studies
and Planning at MIT, and in this video
we will learn about the political economy
of climate action. This video will focus on the international framing
of climate change in particular. When I ask students, when did climate change
become a scientific problem? Most think about this question for a moment
and respond that the issue really arose in the late '60s
and '70s as a byproduct of the environmental movement
in the United States, or that it became a political reality
in the '80s and '90s. Some of them reflect on the Rio Convention in 1992
as the starting point, and in some respects,
these answers are absolutely correct. Climate change became a matter of concern
in the late '80s and the 1990s, but it's not
because this is when the science began
to understand the phenomenon. The science is actually much older. Some point to Joseph Fourier, who
was investigating the greenhouse gas
effect between 1824 and 1827 as the original discoverer. Some suggest it was John Tyndall, who
was doing experiments on identifying carbon
dioxide as the driver of the greenhouse gas
effect in the 1860s and 1870s. Some point to Eunice Foote, who discovered
the phenomenon earlier than John Tyndall,
but because she was a woman and an American,
didn't get credit for her work. So the science behind climate change
has been around for at least 150 years,
and over time it's become much more precise and much
more consolidated under bodies like the Intergovernmental
Panel on Climate Change. But it's been known for a long time. The reason in which people shift climate change
as a matter of concern to the 1990s this
is because it is when it became a development agenda. So this image you see not September 11, 1989,
because, of course, this is protesters standing
in front of the Berlin Wall on November 9, 1989
as East Germans and West Germans alike
were tearing down the wall. It's the beginning of the collapse of the Soviet Union,
and it is this moment in history in this geopolitical reality
that allows leaders around the world to begin
to care about climate change. The 1992 United Conference on Environment and Development,
which was held at Rio de Janeiro,
is what arguably started the United Nations Framework
Convention on Climate Change.

The 1992 United Conference on Environment and Development,
which was held at Rio de Janeiro,
is what arguably started the United Nations Framework
Convention on Climate Change. But the reason was that a new development policy was needed. Prior to this point in time and after the Second World War,
the world had been divided into three spheres of influence
with the United States and their allies
forming what was called the First World, the Soviet
Union and their allies forming what was called the Second
World, and everywhere else everywhere in between all
of the developing countries and former colonial states called
the Third World. The Third World is where the Cold War was actually fought
and where the war was not so cold in places
like that like Afghanistan, South Korea, Vietnam. And in that time, development policy
focused on a single goal--
anything but communism, because there
was a fear that if any one of these developing countries
fell to communism, Vietnam, Laos, Cambodia,
they would all topple like dominoes and the United States
would lose its foothold in combating the Soviet Union
and its socialist ideology. In outlining this new geopolitical reality,
President George H Bush took to the Rio Conference
a set of objectives and goals which you can watch in the link
below. He said he was determined to protect the environment
and the US taxpayer. And here he laid the agenda of linking
environmental protection with economic preservation,
economic integration, and the spread of the dominance
that the United States and Europe had established
with the end of the Cold War. This new world order would foster environmental protection
and economic growth, and he would
seek a climate convention that would increase energy
efficiency and cleaner air. At the heart of it would be technology
cooperation, which would help distribute American technology
to clean up the world's environment. It would also use United States science
to increase the understanding of the climate phenomenon. And then President Bush integrated issues
like his Forest for the Future Initiative, which eventually
became reducing emissions from deforestation and degradation. And then finally, he went to Rio with the conviction
that environmental protection and a growing economy
are inseparable, and ultimately, the world
would take a stake in a strong American economy.

And then finally, he went to Rio with the conviction
that environmental protection and a growing economy
are inseparable, and ultimately, the world
would take a stake in a strong American economy. This type of policy was possible in the United Nations Framework
Convention on Climate Change because at this point
in time, the United States and Europe
saw no challenges, no real economic threats
to their supremacy. And so they established the United Nations Framework
Convention on Climate Change with several key principles
and goals. The first was the aim of having developed parties establish
their greenhouse gas emissions, which included carbon dioxide
and other anthropogenic greenhouse gases not regulated
under the Montreal Protocol at 1990 levels by the year 2000. Much of the actual framework itself borrowed
from the Montreal Protocol, which
was used to eliminate the ozone holes above the Arctic
and Antarctica because of the success of that protocol
on the international stage. And so even the way in which the greenhouse gases
were established and estimated was based off
of what was written into the Montreal Protocol. There were other key and critical objectives. One was article two. This was the objective that the world
would stabilize greenhouse gas concentrations
in the atmosphere at a level that
would prevent dangerous anthropogenic interference
with the climate system. This was incredibly positive in that it set this initiative
on the world stage. It made it a common principle, but somewhat
problematic in that there actually
wasn't a clear definition of what it meant to stabilize
greenhouse gas concentrations, what that would look like,
and what it would mean to prevent
dangerous anthropogenic interference. What does danger mean varies for different people
in different places around the world. What's tolerable for people living in the Great Lakes
region of the United States, for example,
is very different from what's tolerable for people
living on low lying nation states like the Maldives. And then article three was at the heart
of the magnanimous policy that the United States and Europe
were able to set because they were victorious in the Cold
War, the idea of common but differentiated responsibility. This is the idea that all of the developed countries
had already had the chance to industrialize.

This is the idea that all of the developed countries
had already had the chance to industrialize. They were responsible for the greatest share of greenhouse
gas emissions, and so they should ultimately
bear the burden of reducing greenhouse gas emissions. And so they established two tracks of countries. The first were Annex I, or developed
countries, who would all adopt common and equitable binding
targets. The targets would be set based on the ratio
of the size of the country's economy,
as well as the percentage of the global share of greenhouse gas
emissions for which it was responsible. Non-Annex I countries, developing countries,
would not be required to take binding targets. This was the heart of the agreement,
and it was shared between the European Union, which
had been newly formed under the Maastricht Treaty,
to provide economic stability and prosperity in Europe
and the United States. And in 1992, even 1994, it was viable
because it shared the leadership of both the United
States and the European Union.

JANELLE KNOX-HAYES: Hello. My name is Janelle Knox-Hayes. I'm a Professor in the Department of Urban Studies

by the Conference of Parties, and the Conference of Parties
are both the supreme decision making body of the convention. They review the implementation of the convention
and they take decisions necessary to implement
the convention. The COP includes a secretariat and various formal
administrative bodies. It also represents a series of conferences
that are held twice a year, once in June
and once in November or December of every year. The meetings in June are administerial
and they are used to guide the development of protocols
and the negotiations that take place
between ministers in December. Not every year is equally significant,
but within the last 30 years, there
have been major years for the Conference of Party. The first was in 1997 the Kyoto Protocol,
because this is where the first binding
targets were established between Annex I parties. The next significant conference is the 2009 Copenhagen
conference because this is where parties
were meant to agree the extension of the Kyoto

and so ultimately waited until 2015 with the Paris Agreement

a framework that would replace the Kyoto Protocol. More recently in 2019, the conference
was meant to take place in Santiago,

the parties were finally able to establish

So in 2021, which took place in Glasgow,

adaptation. There are several critical principles
in each of the protocols. In the 1997 Kyoto Protocol, which
is adopted by the COP in December 1997
and it opened for signature in 1998,
but not ratified until 2005, the principle
of the common but differentiated responsibilities
was formalized. Annex I countries included OECD developed countries

for Annex I parties, which is a percent reduction below 1990
levels between the final commitment
period of 2008 and 2012. And then Article 25, the double threshold
was important because it was required to bring countries
into the treaty. And this basically said that if I take action,
but my neighbors don't, I ultimately
won't be held to the targets that I set. So to come into force, at least 55 Annex I countries,
the majority representing at least 55% of 1990 level
Annex I emissions must ratify the protocol. The countries all agreed 1990 would be the base level,
because this was the year before the collapse of the Soviet
Union. And so in 1992 and 1998, this was the highest level peak
emissions relative to where the world was
with its current carbon output. And then there were three flexible mechanisms,
the international emissions trading, the clean development
mechanism, and joint implementation. There were a number of enforcement mechanisms
in the Kyoto Protocol but it was challenging
because it's difficult to get countries to accept
international agreements. So in particular, there was an enforcement branch,
which is a quasijudicial process similar to World Trade
Organization dispute settlement that
ensured that penalties were imposed
on non-compliant parties.

So in particular, there was an enforcement branch,
which is a quasijudicial process similar to World Trade
Organization dispute settlement that
ensured that penalties were imposed
on non-compliant parties. And then there was a facilitative branch,
which would advise and facilitate
the implementation of the protocol. The consequence that was written into the enforcement mechanism
for non-compliance was that the enforcement branch
must decide the party's non-compliance
and deduct 1.3 times the excess tons from the parties assigned
amount for the second compliance period. So in effect, if a country failed
to meet their targets under the Kyoto Protocol,
the compliance enforcement branch
would make it even more difficult
for them to meet their targets in the second enforcement
period. They would have a 30% more difficult target
and they would have to try to catch up. And then the party's, the enforcement branch
would request an action plan and suspend
the ability of the country to trade
their assigned allowances, meaning they also couldn't
meet their target by exchanging credits with other countries. In theory and in principle, this was a good approach
because it was meant to signal that countries should
take their target seriously. But in effect, it just meant that when
a number of countries like Japan, Australia
were unable to meet their targets,
they simply left the Kyoto Protocol. And this helped in the collapse of the international system. The other component of the Kyoto Protocol
that was really difficult was ultimately
the United States' lack of willingness
to continue with the idea of common but differentiated
responsibility. When the United States initially agreed to the treaty,

and was instrumental in a series of reforms

And this was a way to tell the Chinese people don't
worry if the economy is actually socialist or capitalist. As long as it's producing growth,
as long as it's enhancing welfare, it's a good economy. And this cleverly allowed China to remain
a socialist or communist country,
but to develop what they called capitalism
with Chinese characteristics. 40 years ago, more than 88% of China's population
lived on less than $2 a day. With Deng Xiaoping's intervention, the creation

market to join the Chinese economy for manufacturing
and production, China ushered in a new era

2001 when it ascended to the World Trade Organization,
it was already a major emerging economy and a clear threat
to the United States. So it's not surprising that in 2001, President George H Bush
withdrew the United States from the Kyoto Protocol. And as you can see in this clip below, at the time,
it was not a matter of party politics. He made clear that the science of climate change
is real, that the United States would still
work with our allies in Europe and in the United Nations
Framework Convention on Climate Change
to establish a better treaty. But ultimately, the US could no longer
accept the principle of common but differentiated
responsibility. President Bush called this flawed. He said that we had to protect the US economy
and we had to protect energy prices. And the administration also had a careful calculation. At that point in time, the US accounted
for 45% of Annex I emissions, which
meant that this should trigger the failure
of the double threshold. It would be virtually impossible for the other developed
countries to get 55 countries to agree in 55% of the annex I's
greenhouse gas emissions because that
would mean they would have to get everyone else. This movement really angered the European Union and European
leaders. They had been trying for 10 years
to establish a common carbon tax policy in Europe
because they had successfully responded
to the threat of ozone depletion with a tax system,
whereas the United States had ultimately
used a market mechanism, the acid rain trading system.

They had been trying for 10 years
to establish a common carbon tax policy in Europe
because they had successfully responded
to the threat of ozone depletion with a tax system,
whereas the United States had ultimately
used a market mechanism, the acid rain trading system. And so they decided because you need unanimity
within the European system to establish a tax,
they would instead try for a market
policy, which only needed a majority
response to achieve policy. And so they created the 2003 EU directive 2003/87/EC,
which established the European Union Emission Trading System. The directive had the aim to contribute
to fulfilling the commitments of the European community
and its member states more effectively
through an efficient European market in greenhouse gas
emission allowances with the least
possible loss of economic development and employment. The establishment of the European Union Emissions
Trading System initially accounted
for 45% of Europe's emissions. It capped the emissions of 10,000 installations, including
power-heavy industry, and established a trading system
with yearly compliance targets that
had ramifications for the rest of the world because of the way
in which the world was trading with Europe. This also critically signal to the business community
that the idea of carbon compliance,
of carbon emissions reduction, would
be a future reality, a future risk that they would have
to contend to, and even without the movement of the United
States, create a global inertial inertia for greenhouse gas
emission reductions. The EU ETS was set up in three phases with caps to tighten

reduction everywhere because it is a global phenomenon. The other idea was that if, for a series of decades,

they would become so much more technologically advanced
and energy efficient that the developing world would never
catch up.

The other idea was that if, for a series of decades,
only Annex I countries participated in the, convention
they would become so much more technologically advanced
and energy efficient that the developing world would never
catch up. The developing world would not be able to participate. So the clean development mechanism
was meant to be a way to engage the developing
world through the creation of offset projects, projects where
the developed world would come in
and share technology would establish
the creation of clean energy through for example,

created a marketplace for all of these projects. So this map, for example, shows the growth and thousands

in places like China, 900 projects in Brazil. And it was a major initiative for the creation

there was the-- the world was ripe for another agreement

a Democratic president, President Barack Obama,

and said he would lead the United States back
into the global arena.

from where they started in Kyoto. The US brought a 26% to 28% reduction against a 2005

PROFESSOR: Environmental justice is
a term that refers to the fair treatment
and meaningful involvement of all people
regardless of race, color, national origin, or income
in relation to the siting, implementation, and enforcement
of environmental and land use laws, regulations,
and policies. It is specifically created to address
the disproportionate environmental burdens
that such decisions have had on low income
and communities of color. Most importantly, environmental justice
is a social movement led by frontline communities who
live in neighborhoods that have been
subject to the injustices associated
with the exposure of noxious uses
and environmental contamination. Stemming from environmental justice,
climate justice is a concept that
addresses the unbalanced distribution of climate change
burdens and the responsibilities that apply to address this,
showing that the communities most affected
are the least connected with the causes of climate change. Both of these movements are rooted
on a definition of justice entailing at least
three important dimensions. First, there is distributive justice
regarding the equitable distribution
of environmental burdens. Second, we have procedural justice
associated with the equal treatment and respect
in decision making processes. And third, there's corrective justice
associated with fairness and punishment with direct input
and oversight from those affected to guarantee
that damages are repaired. True equitable climate action needs to seek justice,
and to do so all of these dimensions need to be met.

have been thinking about these questions. And this is a good conversation to have. These are the right kinds of questions to ask. So looking at the data--

with respect to a human hand. It's about the size of a thimble. It is, again, in solid form. There isn't much waste, in fact, produced from fission,

information to provide people with more power
to make more informed choices before they

information to provide people with more power

especially smog air quality, which
has really adverse impacts on respiratory and cardiovascular
outcomes. Water quality and water quantity are adversely
impacted in ways that are bad for health.

with census blocks indicated. And she developed an index using mortality data,
observed mortality data over a 10-year period
in New York City. And she looked at what are the factors that caused people
to die when it got really hot. And she created an index that accumulated

greater were larger percentage of homes on public assistance,

a greater percent of deaths occurring
at home, greater surface temperatures,

change mitigation, which I think is also an important point
to make, just to remind you that mitigation of climate change
often involves reducing of emissions of greenhouse
pollutants, things like CO2, methane, ozone, black carbon. The sources that emit those pollutants often
also emit other pollutants that are directly
harmful to public health. Actually, black carbon and ozone are health-relevant pollutants. But also you can look at PM2.5, carbon monoxide,
nitrogen oxide, sulfur oxide. These are pollutants that come from many of the same sources
that are carbon sources. And as a result, if we can limit emissions of greenhouse
pollutants, we have the opportunity
to also achieve direct health benefits through improved air
quality. These benefits tend to occur close to where
the emissions are reduced, which really helps
to benefit local populations. And they tend to occur quickly--
the benefits, that is--
offering strong political incentive for local actions

when he launched New York City's sustainability plan,

good for the planet, but it also is good for New Yorkers. Air quality is one way that low-carbon strategies can

other pathways, for example by providing more green space,

reduce air pollution problems, as we, for example,
introduce electric vehicles and reduce transportation
emissions, promoting physical activity through investing
in bicycling and pedestrian infrastructure, which
can have knock-on effects that are beneficial

increasing access to healthy foods

have less mobility, and often less education. Minorities similarly tend to live in older homes,
also have mobility issues, they often lack AC or air purifiers,
and they experience both low income and low education. And then pregnant women and infants,
so there's some very important biological developmental
windows here, and the experience of being pregnant
has been looked at as an underlying
condition for other health outcomes
and the different environmental exposures associated with them. So recently, in the last few years,
the National Academies of science
hosted a workshop that was devoted
to the subject of wildfires and human health. And from those proceedings some major gaps were highlighted,
and we've talked about them a little bit,
but some of these gaps include an understanding
of wildfire impact on indoor air pollution. Also, exposure to smoke as a risk factor for other diseases,
and then again, mental health issues, pregnancy, children. An additional research frontier is the understanding
of smoke exposure from prescribed burning.

Also, exposure to smoke as a risk factor for other diseases,
and then again, mental health issues, pregnancy, children. An additional research frontier is the understanding
of smoke exposure from prescribed burning. If you're not familiar with it, prescribed burning,
in many parts of the country, is used as a source
of fire suppression. However, little is known about the health
impact of these fires compared to wildfires,
so much more needs to be done in this area. So let's take a moment to review what
we've learned, we now understand what the fire triangle is,
and that there are different types of fire,
like wildfire versus prescribed fire. We've established that climate change can greatly
impact this future system with implications for human health. And then we've talked about some of the next steps
to better understand this risk, which
include looking specifically at things like prescribed fire,
the mental health impact of wildfire exposure,
and adverse pregnancy related outcomes that may be linked
to wildfire smoke exposure. So as the urban populations impacted by smoke exposure
continues to grow under climate change
and are expanding urban areas, there's
a significant need to continue research
on the human impact from exposure. So thank you for joining me today
to talk about the impact of climate change
on urban populations due to wildfire smoke exposure. You can find additional information
on wildfire smoke exposure via the EPA, CDC,
and American Lung Association websites. Thank you very much.

yards and informal mechanics, alongside the presence

All of these make Isipingo one of the most depressed
secondary towns in the Durban area. The Isipingo LAP was subjected to an analysis of climate change
projections for the year 2050,
under a business-as-usual and a best-case scenario. Findings showed that substantial warming
will be experienced at least in the Isipingo central business
district and the industrial zone. Through a collaborative session with local stakeholders,
opportunities and challenges of the LAP have been discussed,
and the community priorities mapped. These aspects became the starting point for co-design sessions
with representatives from city planning
department and river basin authority,
providing a portfolio of design solutions
and highlighting their potential socioeconomic environmental
co-benefits. Green and blue infrastructure as well as climate-resilient design
solutions at the building scale were suggested for critical heat
spots in the central business district and the industrial zone. Two key areas were identified for wetland rehabilitation
to attenuate flooding, the transit camp south of the city
and the container depot built
on top of a wetland, restoring the canal system
to revive the estuary ecosystem, alongside the development
of new recreation and ecotourism opportunities. The main takeaway of the experience was the clear demand from technicians
of improving their knowledge of tools integrating modeling,
simulation and collaborative design. It led to new workshops on remote sensing image processing
with the support of NASA in 2020,
and a second round of the Durban UDCW with the support
of Ground Truth for the CICLIA Transformative River Management
Program in January 2022. Another interesting example of the UDCW methodology

a set of multi-scale planning and design support tools
to support climate action in the city has been developed. The goal was to have a coherent reference
framework across the different scale of urban planning, from the
strategic level, with the sustainable climate and energy action
plan, being Napoli among the signatories of the Covenant of Mayors, to
city planning, and district design.

The goal was to have a coherent reference
framework across the different scale of urban planning, from the
strategic level, with the sustainable climate and energy action
plan, being Napoli among the signatories of the Covenant of Mayors, to
city planning, and district design. Locally downscaled climate projections
of extreme events and seasonal trends,
reference hazard/impact scenarios that
take into account urban microclimates,

and guidelines for project revision and implementation,
have been integrated into official planning documents. The UDCW supported in particular the redevelopment
project of a district located in the east suburb of the city. Here, a series of collaborative mapping and co-design exercises,

JANELLE KNOX-HAYES: Hello. I'm Janelle Knox-Hayes. I'm an associate professor in the Department of Urban Studies
and Planning at MIT. And in this video, we'll discuss the climate action plan. Climate action plans are strategic framework documents
used to guide cities in addressing impacts of climate
change in their communities. CAPs are a crucial tool for tracking and meeting
global climate goals. They can be applied at a wide range
of different institutional levels,
from city governments to universities
to federal programs. A climate action plan helps cities mitigate and adapt
to rapid environmental changes, many of which
are regionally and locally specific. The plan can also help businesses and the public gain
awareness of how their activities and actions are
connected to collaborative efforts to mitigate and adapt
to climate change. Climate action plans typically include targets
for reducing greenhouse gas emissions and detailed steps
for meeting and tracking those targets. Plans also often include elements
such as resilience strategies and clean energy targets
and generally focus on implementing actions
that will achieve emissions reductions in the most
cost-effective way possible. Federal governments and local municipalities
develop and utilize climate action plans
as roadmaps for informed decisions,
to better understand where and how to achieve the most
impactful emissions reductions that are
in alignment with other resilience and equity goals. In developing climate action plans,
there are many different approaches
taken by different cities, as CAPs
must be tailored to a city's specific needs
and circumstances. Critical approaches, however, typically include
a data analysis process to collect information
on existing conditions and build an evidence base
to inform policy, dedicated stakeholder engagement
in developing the CAP, and identifying priority
directions for action, and scenario planning
to support the selection of appropriate mitigation
and adaptation strategies to integrate into the CAP. As the C40 Cities states, climate action plans ultimately
help cities to deliver integrated and inclusive plans
that address the need to reduce greenhouse gas emissions,
adapt to the impacts of climate change,
and deliver wider social and economic benefits.
